questions.question: Which big cat can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h faster than most sports cars — going from a standing start to highway speed in just three seconds?
questions.reasoning: Concrete car comparison kids understand viscerally; clean audio delivery and a single-word answer.

questions.question: Sloths move so slowly that something amazing actually grows in their fur — what is it?
questions.reasoning: Gross-cool factor + narrative framing; sparks 'is the sloth ok?' follow-ups.

questions.question: Whether a baby crocodile hatches as a boy or a girl is decided by something kids can easily measure with a thermometer — what is it?
questions.reasoning: Genuinely counter-intuitive biology fact with clean phrasing and a single answer.

questions.question: Polar bears look snow-white, but if you could shave one (please don't!) you'd find their skin underneath is a very surprising color. What color is it?
questions.reasoning: Classic visual contradiction; one-word answer and instantly memorable.

questions.question: What do you call a group of crows when they're all hanging out together — a flock, a herd, or a murder?
questions.reasoning: Three-option scaffold reads cleanly aloud; 'murder' word triggers playful surprise.

questions.question: If you weighed everything in the solar system on a giant scale, the Sun by itself would account for what surprising share of the total — about half, about three-quarters, or over 99 percent?
questions.reasoning: Strong fact but multi-option setup with three abstract percentages stretches audio cognitive load. Consider tightening to two options or making the comparison concrete (e.g., 'about as much as one slice of pie or the whole pie?').

questions.question: Astronauts come back from a few months on the space station having grown a few centimeters TALLER. Why does this happen?
questions.reasoning: Strong narrative hook with body-relatable answer; sparks discussion of gravity and growth.

questions.question: True or false: a single day on the planet Venus is actually LONGER than a whole year on Venus.
questions.reasoning: Breaks an axiomatic 'days fit in years' assumption; T/F is audio-perfect.

questions.question: We're born with about 300 of one body part, but by the time we're grown up, we only have 206. Some of them fuse together! What body part is it?
questions.reasoning: Counter-direction (we lose parts as we grow) is the hook; audible numbers are short.

questions.question: Just like fingerprints, every person on Earth has a totally unique pattern on this part of their body — the part you stick out when you make a silly face!
questions.reasoning: 'Silly face' hint adds playful framing; trivial cognitive load.

questions.question: The Moon is slowly drifting away from Earth — about how much further away does it get every year: a millimeter, a few centimeters, or a meter?
questions.reasoning: Three-unit options work fine audibly; 'fingernail growth' tie-in helps retention.

questions.question: An engineer was walking his dog and noticed something prickly clinging to its fur. He looked at it under a microscope and it inspired one of the most useful sticky inventions ever — what did he invent?
questions.reasoning: Story-driven invention origin; biomimicry angle is strong for kids.

questions.question: Carrots haven't always been orange. The first cultivated carrots, hundreds of years ago, were a totally different color — what was it?
questions.reasoning: Reverses a kindergarten-default association; one-word answer.

questions.question: If the world's smallest country is the Vatican, which country is the BIGGEST in pure land area?
questions.reasoning: Most kids guess Russia immediately and the contrast scaffolding does little extra work. Revise: turn it into a 'fits how many of X inside?' surprise (e.g., 'Russia is so big you can fit how many United States inside it?').

questions.question: Honeybees can do something that scientists thought only smart mammals could do — they can recognize and remember individual ones of these. What?
questions.reasoning: Reverses 'small brain = simple animal' assumption; great car-discussion fact.

questions.question: Microwave ovens were invented by complete accident — when an engineer noticed something in his pocket suddenly melted near a radar machine. What was in his pocket?
questions.reasoning: Iconic accidental-discovery story; clean answer space.

questions.question: If you could fold a normal sheet of paper in half 42 times, surprisingly, how thick would it become — taller than a house, taller than a mountain, or all the way to the Moon?
questions.correct_answer: All the way to the Moon
questions.reasoning: Top-tier exponential-growth wow fact with three concrete options; only docked DrF for the implicit math leap.

questions.question: Octopuses are surprisingly clever — they can even open which everyday human object that has a screwed-on lid?
questions.reasoning: Concrete domestic object as the punchline makes octopus intelligence visceral.

questions.question: Some sea turtles alive today belong to a family that's older than something else amazing — they were swimming around BEFORE which famous extinct creatures appeared?
questions.reasoning: 'Older than dinosaurs' pattern is timeless kid-bait; clean single-word answer.

questions.question: Mount Everest is so tall it's still growing — about how much taller does it get every year: a millimeter, a few millimeters, or a meter?
questions.reasoning: Concept is good but the three options span only mm/m and audibly the 'millimeter vs few millimeters' distinction is subtle. Revise: pair with the underlying tectonic story ('two giant continents pushing into each other') instead of three near-identical options.
